input.background: Exercises 13.1, 13.3, and 13.5 considered birth weight in four groups of mothers. Table 13.3 lists the data. Data are also stored online in the file SMOK_BW.SAV. The ANOVA test found significant evidence against $H_0 (P=0.042)$. Now apply a Kruskal-Wallis test. Show all hypothesis-testing steps. Are the Kruskal-Wallis results consistent with the ANOVA results?
input.question: Apply a Kruskal-Wallis test and show all hypothesis-testing steps.

output.answer:
A. Hypotheses. $H_0$ : birth weight distributions in the four populations are equal in location versus $H_2$ : the birth weight in at least one of the populations differs
B. Test statistic. Chi-square $=7.305$ with $3 d f$ (Calculated with SPSS, Rel. 11.0.1. 2001. Chicago: SPSS Inc.)
C. $ oldsymbol{P}$-value. $P=0.063$

birth_weight.value: Values of birth weights from four groups of mothers
birth_weight.description: Birth weight of infants categorized by the group of mothers

mother_group.description: Categorical variable indicating the group of mothers

meta_info.caption: Smoking and birth weight, Kruskal-Wallis test.
